title: Introduction to Infographics
summary: Traces the history of infographics from early data journalism to contemporary interactive visualisations. Introduces four conceptual frameworks for understanding infographics and key standards of design.
content_html:
Introduction to Infographics

An infographic is, at its most basic level, a combination of information (info) and design (graphics). Infographics are one key way in which data is communicated to wider audiences.

Data Journalism: Background

Data journalism is grounded in a larger quantitative turn within journalism (Petre, 2013). Current data journalism has deep democratic roots (Coddington, 2014) and evolved from computer-assisted reporting (CAR). CAR branched into related practices including computational journalism, open-source journalism, and data journalism.

As Paul Bradshaw defines it: "Data can be the source of data journalism, or it can be the tool with which the story is told — or it can be both. Like any source, it should be treated with scepticism; and like any tool, we should be conscious of how it can shape and restrict the stories that are created with it."

A Brief History of Infographics

Bogost et al. (2010) traces visual data representation from the 1930s onwards — from maps, to economic and statistical charts, to contemporary "chartoons" popularised by Nigel Holmes in the 1960s–70s. The key shift has been from static print infographics to interactive data visualisations.

Segal and Heer (2010) describe seven genres of narrative visualisation: magazine style, annotated chart, partitioned poster, flow chart, comic strip, slide show, and video.

British Standard Chart Types

Dick (2016) lists standard infographic types including: table, bar graph, line graph, area graph, pie graph, isotope graph, scatter graph, histogram, three-dimensional graph, superimposed graph, thematic map, and illustrated graph.

Four Frameworks for Infographics

Dick (2016) outlines four ways infographics are typically discussed:

  • Functionalist-idealist — communicating truth through data; visualisations as scientific methodology; more common in statistics and maths. Does not account for contested ideas of 'truth'.
  • Pragmatist-realist — infographics as a tool or technology rather than communication; considers practical constraints of the publishing medium.
  • Expressionist-aesthete — infographics as design; emphasis on expressive experimentation, aesthetics, and playful design.
  • Didactic-persuasive — infographics as education or persuasion; the function is to teach or convince; historically rooted in pictographs. Criticised for being overly reductive.

These categories can overlap — a functionalist infographic can still be playful. They are useful for grouping ways of thinking about the production process and categorising specific audiences and intentions.

Chartjunk

Coined by Edward Tufte, chartjunk describes redundant data or visual elements that do not convey meaning. A good data visualisation should not contain chartjunk.

exercises.title: Data Journalism Origins
exercises.prompt: What reporting practice did data journalism evolve from?

exercises.title: Infographic Frameworks
exercises.prompt: Which framework views infographics primarily as a tool or technology, focusing on practical constraints of the publishing medium?

exercises.title: Chartjunk
options: Redundant visual elements that do not convey meaning
Charts that are too complex to understand
exercises.answer: Redundant visual elements that do not convey meaning

exercises.title: Narrative Visualisation Genres
exercises.prompt: Which of the following is one of Segal and Heer's (2010) seven genres of narrative visualisation?
